My wife died a few weeks ago. She came to me a few days after her death in a dream to say goodbye, and then last night she came to me again. I've had quite a few post death goodbye dreams, but I've never had two post death goodbye dreams, and the second one is profoundly strange, strange enough I want to post it here.

Like they normally are, this one was in a travel station. They're normally small (think a bus stop or small airport), but this one looked like it was in a major airport. When I commented on it, she said it was because she was going a lot further than the dead normally do, and there were a lot of people going with her. She then said she wanted to say goodbye, because she didn't think we would see each other ever again, not even in future lives, and she wanted to thank me for making her time here a lot better than it could have been.

She told me that several thousand years ago she got stuck in our world due to a huge accident. I didn't ask and she didn't give details, so this is all I have to go on, and now I wish I had asked for more. Now, though, she was finally able to go back to her home. As we were talking, a couple of jumbo jets landed in the distance, and when I commented on just how many people those could carry, she told me there were about 2 billion or so souls who came with her, and almost all of them were going home. She didn't know the exact details, but what she'd been told was that they started setting up and began bringing a small number of souls back around 10-15 years ago, that the pace really started picking up in mid-August, and it will run full steam for the next few years before it will wrap up completely about a decade from now.

At this point I asked about going with her, and she said it was a very bad idea. Her native form is utterly alien to human beings; in addition to bodies that are more like a form of energy than a physical body, they have a social organization like ants, so the way our two forms think, feel, and live are so different that it is profoundly unpleasant for a soul meant for one form to be in the other; she then told me there was nothing about our world she would miss besides some loved ones, and if I went with her she didn't even think I'd be able to get that comfort. She also said if I went with her it would be several thousand years before I could come home once I realized it was a mistake (and she was certain I would), and so it was best if I stayed here. This way I could also take care of our family, because she didn't think any of them would be coming with her either.

At this point there was a call for boarding, so she got up, gave me a hug, said she loved me and thanked me for everything, and then began walking towards the gate. She showed her ID to something that seemed almost but not quite human, and then walked through what looked like some sort of mist right at the entrance to the gate. As the mist hit her she turned into a shimmering thing that looks like nothing on Earth and that I'm not sure I can describe no matter how hard I try. As the thing descended the ramp I ran after her, willing to take the risk of being stuck in some alien form if it meant being with her, but a gate agent blocked me, looked at my ID, and said he was very sorry, but that I had to stay. Then the dream ended as I woke up in a cold sweat, and I somehow knew this really was goodbye forever.

Taken at face value it suggests billions of alien souls have been stuck here for millennia, something that sounds insane; and I don't know if it should be taken at face value or not. I want to dismiss it for a galaxy of reasons, but it makes sense: I know my wife was always "off". I love her deeply, but sometimes she would say and do things that left me feeling very cold, and I knew that no matter how hard I tried she just never felt comfortable in her own skin. She even said sometimes that she felt like she was an alien forced to pretend to be human for so long she forgot what she really was, and somehow I'm not surprised there appears to have been truth to that feeling.

If this is accurate, then it seems likely there will be a lot of other people seeing this sort of thing, which could be a useful test. Unfortunately because my wife died at close to the exact time she told me in the dream that this crossing over picked up pace, keeping up with open posts, Magic Mondays, and other areas where this sort of discussion might happen during this time has not been on my mind. As a result, I'm not sure if anyone else has reported anything like this yet, but I will be keeping an eye out in case people start reporting it. Either way, I want to let you know in case there is something to it, because if there are a couple billion souls who don't belong here but have been stuck here for thousands of years and are now leaving, that implies we are in for a pretty wild set of changes in the very near future.

Demons don't really have a plane of their own -- they're crouched in the rubble at the very lowest levels of the plane of dense matter, where the broken remnants of dead universes wind up in the process of creation. Weirdly enough, Clark Ashton Smith seems to have had a vision of that subplane, and put it into one of his stories:

http://eldritchdark.com/writings/short-stories/2/the-abominations-of-yondo

But, yes, according to many occult authors, now and again one of them manages to get a human body and blunders destructively through the human world for what usually amounts to a very short lifetime -- neither the human world nor their own bodies make sense to them, and so they almost always die young.

I knew someone once, when I worked in a nursing home many years ago, who may have been one of them. She was a woman of around thirty, dying of extreme complications of diabetes; we were warned to be careful around her, as she'd tried to murder both her sisters. She was unusually tall, awkwardly shaped, and clumsy; she never showed the least trace of conscience, self-control, or the ability to foresee the consequences of her actions, and her sole pleasure was tormenting those of the staff who tried to be nice to her. The home I worked in got rid of her after two weeks by sending her to the hospital and then quickly filling the bed with another patient, so I never did find out what happened to her.

replies.content:
Rather, "there's no need to risk accusing..."

I have an apocalyptic view of the mid-to-near future, but my hackles are still up about this in particular. I'm mostly expecting to be rugpulled (about this, not mostly the apocalypticism) one way or another. This kind of psychosocial-local-causal-environment-relative synchronicity pileup thing seems to me to be such a pervasive feature of the supernatural, and of how it interacts with material processes of meaning-perception, that I don't even really think of it as being worth calling out details in hypotheticals about recognizably distinct trickster entities doing the job. (You could still do it, but it seems like mostly they ought more to be called "superficial-aspects-of-meaningfulness elementals" than tricksters as such; it's not like there's any obvious reason they couldn't later instead get hired on to help coordinate synchronicities involving well-grounded meaningfulness instead.) It'd be more like those incomplete mile-high baroque spire productions in Solaris, that are full of compelling coherently consistent detail, but only affect a local area of the superorganism and then collapse after a few hours or days, and feel basically like a blind natural phenomenon. Or like intentional communities sprouting like mushrooms after a sufficiently bracing countercultural downpour, and then failing to achieve coherent economic sustainability.

I think the discrepant thing here, that people have gotten semantic saturation about and stopped noticing, is that there's not some other community that is in view of us in general, that could be be trusted to notice and process this sort of thing if it was real and the dreams/visions were sprinkled in an unbiased way over potential recipients, enough that even just the people in the funnel for ending up commenting about it here would have noticed. So nobody is going like, "hey, let's go check /r/occult", or "at least we'll get a clearer picture a few months from now from the field reports and epidemiological surveys collated by the Journal of Unfortunately This Journal Doesn't Exist (and In Fact Probably Just Can't Exist Even If You Wanted It To)". That's the discrepancy: that such an alternative doesn't obviously exist.

I mean, if there was such a larger cultural structure, then surely it would interact with whatever the constraint is that prevents the existence of stable timelines in which there is evidence of the supernatural that is not somewhat-readily interpret-away-able to some cognitive processes causally downstream of that evidence -- probably, to the cognitions of the fraction of the general population who is still doing the initiation of the nadir (and, if not that, then probably to some equivalent of Steiner's spider-creatures). The closest thing we have to such a structure in general is the Catholic Church investigating reports of miracles, and I'm not sure it takes all that much remove from them for the relevant processes to interpret-away the Church's findings as some generalization of "ah, they're probably just misattributing remission of lupus again or something". In the present case, the problem is that such visions and dreams arising independently in sufficient quantities, without a plausible material cause, would be harder to interpret away, especially if the experiences were collated and documented properly.

The not-even-noticing of the discrepancy of the nonexistence of such a reliable external community, then, is sort of part of a broader pattern of no-longer-even-noticing of this other discrepancy: of how evidence of the supernatural that is not at least somewhat-readily interpret-away-able doesn't exist, even if you would totally have expected bulletproof evidence to have existed, if you naively extrapolated the bottom-up implications of the superficially apparent causal patterns of the effects of the supernatural, without awareness of top-down constraints.

If you haven't noticed this pattern, I'm not sure how to explain it. It's kind of like how if you were watching a PG-rated television show or movie and reasoning naively about bottom-up internal causal patterns, you might expect that on the uncommon occassions where characters happened to have their clothes off for some reason, there'd be no bottom-up, causal reason to expect correlation between that event and the locations of obscuring foliage, props, darkness, or the edge of the screen. But somehow the naughty bits are always obscured from the camera, because of the unexpected outside-context-problem of the existence of a top-down, "acausal" constraint preventing their visibility. (Or, in-universe, they are at least always obscured from at least one of the places where the camera would be.) In situations where one had some other reason to doubt the existence of such a top-down constraint, it'd be all too easy to reason, like: "Naughty bits don't exist. If they did exist, we totally would have had been able to engineer a situation that would have produced bulletproof evidence by this point; it's been decades and decades. Instead, we just get these consistent ambiguous points that just generate dispute (and ill-will). Responsible extrapolation from other available evidence compels the default judgement that those patches of flesh are just smooth. Extraordinary claims require extraordinary evidence", or something.

(In this scenario, metaphorically, it seems to me to be a bad vice to constantly confidently derogate the naughty-bits-unbelievers for honestly(?) trying to build out a consistent worldview of what it would have to mean if naughty bits didn't exist, and for trying to develop and act on whatever an ethos of making a such world a better place would consequently counterintuitively have to look like... and now I'm thinking of those doujin communities that used to painstakingly go through and uncensor manga or anime... I guess this metaphor kind of got away from me.)

The best-case realistic scenario, in my eyes, is that the dreams and visions and such convey some kind of obscure mythical truth about some coming civilizational disruptions, but are superficially extremely obtuse, both with respect to those disruptions, and with respect to any of the relatively direct ways of rendering the "straightforward" supernatural significance of those disruptions into the sorts of intelligible concepts that one might otherwise have naively expected. That way, other communities can get other extremely obtuse mythical truths about the same thing, and everyone can get some minimal amount of relevant guidance, while still allowing for there to be enough contradictions and baffling inconsistencies to prevent anything from existing that someone in the relevant subpopulation or whatever might get cornered into not being able to interpret-away.

Let's put aside the question of whether the vision is true or not for a moment, and ask what it implies if adopted as true. In particular, what actions should each person take if this idea is true?

Well, anyone who has a sense of not belonging here should commit suicide, because there's no point remaining here anymore; but first, they should also at least consider killing their loved ones, just in case they are in the category of "might leave or might go depending on time of death" that some speculation has suggested exists. It seems to me it would be easier to cross over with your loved ones, because then you're leaving less behind! If someone with one of these souls should choose to remain alive, while there's some point to worrying about the consequences of your actions from karmic perspectives, it would be awfully tempting to argue that since it sounds like they don't have free will like we do, they shouldn't be held responsible for the consequences of their actions here, and I worry some people will decide they are sixth plane souls to try to ignore the consequences of their actions. I also worry a lot of people who do belong here, but are reacting to a sense of not belonging in a specific place will end up getting caught up here. I had that feeling myself, because what I needed to do in this life required I cross the Atlantic (long story short, my last life fled across the Atlantic to flee from something, and I needed to undo that mistake); I never belonged in North America, because I belong in Europe. Had I embraced this vision I would have stopped looking for where I belonged, and probably severely hindered my soul's growth.

Anyone, whether we belong here or not, should stop worrying about problems such as overpopulation because that problem will solve itself, which implies giving up on worries about things such as housing costs; in fact, a sharp decline in the world's population will improve economic prospects generally, so we don't need to do anything except wait for the die off. Someone even presented a plausible argument for expecting most of the souls who do belong here to leave incarnation for a long while shortly after the alien souls leave too, so a strong case could be made that accepting this idea justifies ignoring the longer term (anything more than 10 years, 15 at most) consequences of our actions, because we will almost certainly be dead; and a die off of that scale will transform the world so completely that there's no point in trying to plan for what comes next.

As for the souls who remain, what's it imply for us? Well, first of all, we're not going to have to do any soul-searching to figure out why humanity made the catastrophic mistakes of the last few centuries; that was entirely an alien and inhuman influence! It was not us, no sir! It's a wonderful way to avoid taking responsibility for our collective actions entirely.

And perhaps most troubling of all, since it looks like under at least certain circumstances this influence can linger if the body of someone with one of these alien souls lives long enough, this vision implies that we could make the future more human by ensuring that all of them die. In other words this vision and the set of attendant speculation suggests that mass murder is justified.

Since if this vision is true certain political movements (such as Progressivism, Communism, and arguably the entire political Left) are heavily shaped by these souls, it is now not just justifiably but arguably moral to go around murdering people for their political views! Likewise with religions: Jews, Christians, Muslims, Buddhists, all belong to religions shaped by these alien souls; whether any individual follower of those faiths is or not, well, better safe than sorry, and now that I think about it, either way, we probably do want to root out their toxic and inhuman egregors.

I think any idea that leads directly to "You should at least consider murdering your family and then killing yourself" and "Mass murder of people for political and religious views is morally justified and arguably obligatory" is an extremely dangerous idea to take seriosuly indeed.

No, these extreme conclusions don't follow at all, unless you also assume that the afterlife is a complete free-for-all in which actions have no consequences. Exactly the opposite teaching is held by every religion I know of, and also by every traditional occult school. (This is why, for example, near-death experiencers and deathbed visionaries around the world see welcoming committees waiting for them -- that takes some organization, you know!) Let's take your proposals and examine them to the nearest approximate metaphor.

Say you're convinced that you're one of the souls from elsewhere, and decide you should kill yourself for that reason. Occult tradition has it that suicides end up in the equivalent of a waiting room until their natural lives run out. So there you sit, while the bus to the sixth Cosmic plane is boarding on the other side of town. It's by no means sure that you'll be able to get there and board. If you'd waited, and you were actually one of the souls scheduled to board, a timely heart attack or car crash would have gotten you there on time...but as it is, you're here for the next 6000 years. Sorry.

Or you're convinced that you're one of the souls from elsewhere, and you kill your family. You get to the bus station, they check your ticket, and all of a sudden you find yourself pulled out of the line. "Sorry, bud," the cop says as he handcuffs you, "but there's a karmic warrant for your arrest." You land in jail as the bus pulls away. The judge brings down the gavel, and you have to serve your karmic sentence -- and that means you're here for the next 6000 years. Too bad, so sad.

Or you get back to the sixth Cosmic plane and you have to go through the equivalent of customs and immigration. They take one look at you and chuck you in a holding cell, because of course the seventh plane authorities let them know what you did. Maybe they send you back to do your time here, or maybe they decide to let you serve your karmic sentence being gnawed on by Denebian slime devils for a hundred lifetimes there, or maybe neither plane wants you and you get chucked out beyond the Ring-Pass-Not to suffer the Unknown Death. So long, sucker.

Or maybe you think you're one of the souls from elsewhere, and you're not, but you do the crimes anyway. The cops haul you in to the judge of the Karmic Court, who looks over her glasses at you and finds you guilty as sin. When you protest that you thought it was a good idea, she tells you that ignorance of the law is no excuse, and condemns you to spend your next fifty lifetimes as a sheep, to help you get over the notion that it's a good idea to run bleating after any idea that makes you feel special. Oh, and every one of those lives will end by being devoured by wolves, so you can work off the karma of murder. Enjoy your pasture, Lambchop.

Of course other scenarios could be invented. My point is that you're making sweeping assumptions that don't follow from the vision itself. I don't think it's a mistake to discuss the vision, especially since other people seem to have had similar inner experiences. I do think it would be a mistake to take it on faith, and it would be an act of epic stupidity on the grand scale to make important life choices on the assumption that it's literally true -- but the possibility that it's symbolically valid, or that its prevalence may teach us something about contemporary consciousness, makes it seem worth exploring to me.

Expats often find themselves experiencing "reverse culture shock" when they return to their home countries - a combination of forgetting the negative aspects of their home culture as well as unawareness of the changes in their home culture since they left (even if they are intellectually aware of the changes from e.g. keeping up with news, they're not viscerally aware). Curiously enough, the standard tables that have been used for centuries by the Eastern Orthodox Churches to compute the (changeable) date of Easter from one year to the next start from the Creation of the World in what is our year 5508 BCE. In these tables, the dates of Easter repeat over a cycle of 532 years. The fourteenth cycle ended in the year 1940 CE, and the fifteenth cycle began in the year 1941, that is, in the year 7449 from the Creation of the Word. This lines up well enough, on a cosmic scale, with the supposed date of the "original SNAFU" some 6000 years ago, more or less.

Also, curiously enough, the year 5000 from the Creation of the World in these tables is, in our calendar, the year 1492 CE, when the European discovery of the Americas changed the course of Western history. As that year approached, Russian theological writers began to speculate about the end of the world and the Second Coming of Christ. One could say that their world actually did begin to end in that year, just not as they had supposed it would.

Make of all this what you will. I find it fascinating, but not compelling.

comments.title: Questions on Transits
comments.content:
So I’ve received my copy of Robert Hand’s Planets In Transit, as you suggested. I find it contains a wealth of knowledge and, I am sure, wisdom. Yet I have some questions to which as yet I find no answers in Mr. Hand’s book.

I) Need a transit be treated in any way as an election is treated? That is, if today’s Mercury is transiting my natal Venus in a perfect way, but Mercury is also currently afflicted by aspect or placement, will those factors adversely affect the transit? If so, how so, and to what degree? Will the transit still be usable?

II) My main goal with this book is to maximize my chances of success with an artistic career. For many years I have been getting my ass stomped having great difficulty in this regard. I will be paying particular attention to transits between Venus, Jupiter, Mercury and perhaps the Sun. Are there any other factors I should be paying attention to? For example, I notice that according to Mr. Hand, Mars can play a rôle in matters of business and success. But my natal Mars is very badly afflicted; can I still safely use Mars transits? (Obviously this last also relates in some degree to question I.)

III) I also have the current task of doing battle with powers and principalities. I mean, I am facing the necessity, onerous though it be, to have dealings with officialdom at the state level, involving bureaucracies like social security and Medicare, etc. I have the task of protecting the interests of myself and another person from possible official predation. Is there any class of transits I should be especially paying attention to?

Of *course* I will be doing my own homework and am already marking up Hand’s book very carefully with these ends in view. But a few pointers might help me to avoid some blunders and help me to save precious time (the official business matters are quite time-sensitive).

IV) Should factors in one’s horoscope affect the approach to transits? For instance my natal Mercury is applying to sextile Venus quite closely, probably the most favorable aspect in my horoscope, while Jupiter and Venus afflict one another (very bad for an artist, I’m sure). Is there anything I should be looking out for in this regard, or do these not matter for transits?

I’m ready, I’m raring to get going on this.

replies.title: Re: Questions on Transits
replies.content:
1) Elections are more fragile than ordinary transits. You wouldn't use an election that had any significant debilities at all, but in a transit, if the dignities outweigh the debilities, you can use it.

2) Use your natal chart as a guide to which planets will work for you and which will work against you. If your Mars is badly afflicted, avoid Mars transits entirely.

3) The 10th house rulers of your natal chart and the other person's natal chart will rule the way that each of you deal with officialdom. Get those rulers in good aspect to benefics and you'll have better luck.

4) Always, always, always start with the natal chart. Your natal chart tells you the hand you've been dealt in this incarnation; transits are just the cards that come your way in the course of the game. I use transits across my natal positions as the foundation of my career timing -- for example, I make sure that something's applying in favorable aspect to my natal Mercury or the ruler of my 2nd or 10th natal houses when contacting a publisher, submitting a manuscript, or anything like that. It was when I started doing that sort of timing that I went from barely making a living to having plenty of money. Your mileage may vary, but it works for me.

replies.title: Re: Questions on Transits
replies.content:
Thanks! This is great. I will reread your remarks numerous times.

None of my natal planets is completely unafflicted, save perhaps the Sun (Aries in the 4th). But pursuant to your comments I notice that the rulers of my Tenth and First Houses are, respectively, Venus and Jupiter: the very two planets whose natal opposition so frustratingly thwarts my ambitions. Perhaps there is a way I can use their House rulerships to in some degree vitiate this problem. If so, do let me know!

replies.title: Re: Questions on Transits
replies.content:
The affliction between Jupiter and Venus, whether it's a square or an opposition, can become a source of strength rather than a hindrance. You need to learn to separate the influences of the two planets and balance them against each other. Doing meditative and/or ritual work with each planet while it's well dignified will help with this -- and, er, Jupiter's exalted right now in Cancer, so this is a good time to start. Explore the Jupiter energy in your life -- its strengths and its weaknesses. Look for ways to increase the former and decrease the latter. If you have planets in good aspect to Jupiter, or in a sign ruled by Jupiter, see if you can use the attitudes and behaviors that correspond to that planet to strengthen and guide your Jupiter energies. Then, when Venus goes into Libra next month, do the same thing with Venus.

Always remember the old Rosicrucian adage: "fools are ruled by their stars, the wise rule theirs." You can turn whatever cards the heavens dealt you into a winning hand. Make it happen.

replies.title: Re: Questions on Transits
replies.content:

comments.title: Magical Practice and Occult Training
comments.content:
Hello! Please forgive me if this has already been answered; I tried searching previous posts.

As I understand it, the Fellowship of the Hermetic Rose and the Golden Section Fellowship are both best understood as systems of occult training, and do not involve a great deal of magic per se.

Are there forms of magical practice that are suitable for practicing concurrently with those systems, or conversely, forms that would best be avoided?

replies.title: Re: Magical Practice and Occult Training
replies.content:
1) That's correct.

2) The magical techniques taught in The Druid Magic Handbook are compatible with both those traditions. So is every form of natural magic I've ever worked with, and every form of astrological magic -- I've done traditional Southern conjure, Renaissance astrological magic, and many other styles of natural and astrological magic while doing SoP-based workings, with good results. On the other hand, the magical techniques taught by the Golden Dawn should be avoided, as they don't play well with Sphere of Protection-based systems.

You can go ahead and ask the other question if you like -- you got this one in quite promptly, after all!

replies.title: Re: Magical Practice and Occult Training
replies.content:

What are the factors that one should consider in choosing between FHR and GSF?

replies.title: Re: Magical Practice and Occult Training
replies.content:
It's pure personal preference. The FHR is closer to the standard Golden Dawn curriculum; the GSF strays into sacred geometry, earth mysteries, and radionics -- think of it as the weirder end of occultism. ;-) They're sufficiently similar that practitioners of both systems can form a lodge together and work the seasonal rituals jointly!

Oh, and of course there's the financial difference. I put the FHR materials out there for free because a lot of people are in tight economic straits these days, and I wanted to make sure that even people who had no money to spare could do serious occult training if they wanted to. The GSF -- well, you have to buy some books and help me pay my rent.

replies.title: Re: Magical Practice and Occult Training
replies.content:
The central work of the FHR curriculum is discursive meditation, and if you run out of themes to meditate on, even after the course is finished, you really aren't trying. In my case, once I learned the original version of the FHR material with John Gilbert, I went on to do a bunch of additional work in the related system of Druidry he taught me, that of AODA, and then completed my work with the Modern Order of Essenes and the Order of Spiritual Alchemy, then did the work that turned into The Druid Magic Handbook, then helped set up the Gnostic Celtic Church, then did the work that turned into The Dolmen Arch. comments.title: Affirmation Help
comments.content:
Hi JMG,

Can I please have some help crafting an affirmation? I want it to help me overcome the habit of watching pornography/masturbation. As I am in my early 30s and in a relationship celibacy isn't an option. I've been thinking about something along the lines of "I am chaste" but I associate that word (chaste) with Catholicism (which I'm not hugely keen on). Any help would be greatly appreciated. Thank you.

replies.title: Re: Affirmation Help
replies.content:
Okay, the first thing you have to do is figure out what's behind the problem. You're in a relationship, you've got a natural and healthy outlet for your sexual energies -- why are you still turning to pornography and masturbation? That's not a rhetorical question; it's something you'll need to figure out if an affirmation is going to do you any good at all. This also isn't an invitation to beat up on yourself -- that's just another form of self-abuse. ;-)

Thus I'd encourage you to start by journaling. Write "why am I still masturbating?" at the top of a sheet of paper and then just let yourself write whatever comes to mind, without censoring or editing anything. What you get will likely be a mix of garbage, pop-culture cliches, and useful insights. Repeat this regularly, varying the question as you wish. Your goal is to find out what your real motivations are. You're not a passive bystander in all this; once you know why you're doing what you're doing, you can take action.

A bit of Rosicrucian energy work may also help. Here's what George Winslow Plummer had to say:

"At such times as sexual desire seems imperative, stand erect, muscles tense, place the hands palms down over the sex organs — partially or, better still, completely disrobed — and while doing so, concentrate on the thought: "I Will that this power ascend to higher regions — that it follow this path — I can feel it so doing," meanwhile drawing the hands up slowly on each side of the abdomen, following the natural curve of the body, until they stop over the nipples or mammary glands. Pause a moment until conscious of a distinct swelling of the breasts, a sensation of energy therein. Practice this on several occasions until the evidence of the upward surging of the vital powers is undoubted. Then, as a step farther, the next time it is practiced, bring the hands up to the throat, until it manifests in a distinct desire to speak with force and vigor. Third step — bring the hands completely up over the back and front brains, and you will become conscious of a power to create in the line of thought or mental process never before experienced. This practice is invaluable to the men or women who use their brains rather than their hands in the regular occupations of life."

Once you have those in process, yes, affirmations will help. May I suggest one that comes from an unexpected angle? "Pornography is boring." Work with that until you realize that it's true, and then see if you need further help. "Masturbation is lonely" can help redirect desires toward your partner, which is after all where they ought to be going.

replies.title: Re: Affirmation Help
replies.content: Thank you very much! This is very helpful.

replies.title: Re: Affirmation Help
replies.content:
How I about something along these lines:

"I direct my sexual energies and desires towards my partner in an appropriate way and channel the rest in my creative endeavors."

You could substitute creative endeavors towards something like work, career, or something you are already doing or plan to do.

Mauve Paradoxical Otter Flaneur

replies.title: Re: Affirmation Help
replies.content: Thank you for your input!

replies.title: Re: Affirmation Help
replies.content:
I recommend crafting any affirmations around those energies and pathways you would like to embrace, master, or reclaim, rather than those energies or pathways you wish could be banished, isolated, or abnegated. Focusing your soul's deepest intentions onto resisting unwanted energies usually just results in them flourishing in your life. After all, what you contemplate you inevitably end up imitating.

The whole abstaining concept of chastity falls into the category of renunciations, resistances, and rejections. Focusing on what you aim to resist is a very potent way to get it locked permanently in place in your life. Instead of battling futilely against "inappropriate" desires, could you focus on rediscovering and reclaiming any "appropriate" desires that your life thus far has left you divorced from accessing? Are you feeling lonely or unwanted or out of place in any way? If so, why? Are there energies or pathways that you have lost access to that you would like to regain access to? What affirmations would help you remember all of who you are, why you're here, what you care about, and how naturally drawn you are to what's appropriate for you?

— Christophe

replies.title: Re: Affirmation Help
replies.content: Thank you Christophe. Much food for thought here. This really is an amazing online community.

comments.title: Fixed Star Talisman

I read parts of Robson's The Fixed Stars and Constellations in Astrology book that you linked to in the last MM.

replies.title: Re: Fixed Star Talisman
replies.content:
1) The fifteen Behenian stars -- the ones that have the seals -- are easiest to invoke, but they're not the only stars that can be used in talismanic magic. Altair is called the Eagle Star -- its name comes from the Arabic al-nasr al-ta'ir, "the Flying Eagle" -- so its talisman should bear the image of an eagle with outspread wings. It is of the nature of Jupiter and Mars, so neither of these planets should be afflicted when the talisman is consecrated; use a mixture of Jovial and Martial incense, and if you make a talisman of watercolor paper (my usual approach), paint the paper in advance with a tea made from herbs of Jupiter and Mars, allow it to dry completely, and then, at the time elected for the consecration, draw the talisman design in ink and suffumigate the talisman while reciting an invocation of the forces of Altair (which you'll have to compose yourself, of course).

2) Good heavens, no. As long as they're not badly afflicted, you can work with fixed stars irrespective of their house placements, though they'll tend to affect affairs of the house in which they're placed. Capella in the 5th will tend to make you lucky in gambling, speculation, and love affairs, and if you have children, they will very likely prosper when they're adults.

3) Like many precious stones and some beautiful people, the Pleiades are lovely but grief and pain follow in their footsteps. Yes, the universe is weird.

replies.title: (no subject)

comments.content:
I was very grateful to you (and Robert Mathieson) for your replies to my question last week about feeling like a chess-piece on the board of life, moved around by invisible forces. If I may, I’d like to follow up that exchange.

First, your phrase about “resolving karmic ties” had a very powerful, almost physical, effect on me. Indeed, it was so powerful that it kept crawling out of my brain and I had to write it down eventually so I could refer to it, as though it was really something I didn’t want to know. So my first question would be, “what’s a karmic tie?” I can imagine involvement with people in past lives, but that doesn’t explain why I have this creepy feeling of being moved around by mysterious forces in pursuit of some design. It seems that for the past fifty years, I’ve been sent off to gather experience, expertise and knowledge, sent somewhere in the world to be useful, brought back for more experience, deployed again and, in the latter stages, doing both in parallel. And it’s not even one entity that’s doing it: it must be half a dozen at least. But why? I’ve been privileged (?) to be part of things that now appear in books, although always in a supporting role, often as an “adviser.” As Robert (and Thomas) said, Ama nesciri et de nihilo reputari, and that has pretty much been the story of my life. I don’t see in what way I can have karmic ties in such circumstances, not least because there’s a clear and as I say rather creepy progression to the whole thing, as a complete process.

Then of course, how do I “resolve” them? Is it just waiting passively for karma to play itself out? Is there something I should be doing? I’m getting older and tireder and no longer jump on aeroplanes with as much enthusiasm as I used to. I’ve started lecturing again, but with less enthusiasm than before. Do I just go on because that’s destiny or karma? Are there any sources for the theories of occult philosophy you mention? Online, there’s a fair amount about resolving karmic ties to individual, and a lot of New Age style courses demanding money, but that’s all. Do you have any further wisdom on the subject?
Many thanks.

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content:
As I see it, resolving karmic ties is not the most important thing to work on in one's life (lives). Living your life (or lives) is not primarily a thing you do for your own sake, or the sake of your friends, relations, ancestors and descendants, but a thing that is done to you by those inconceivable game-players (whom I wrote about) for vast purposes beyond our puny human ability to comprehend anything.

And I, personally, am totally fine with that. I turned 83 a few days ago, and those 83 years, for all the scars they have left on me, have been very much worth living. What makes them so worth living is the impacts I have had on others, which are part of my role as a tiny feeble pawn in that ancient and vast game.

Oh, I know. When Sara and I came back to Seattle after visiting England in 2003, it stunned us how bleak, filthy, and soulless the city had become. We'd both known it from earliest childhood -- I grew up just south of Seattle, her grandparents lived in Seattle and she visited them many times -- and it took the shock of being someplace less toxic for a month to help us see how far it had fallen. It took us a year to make the arrangements but we left in the summer of 2004 and, aside from one brief visit, never went back.

But the forests are quite another matter. They're not just indifferent to human life, the way the ocean is -- the North Pacific has that vibe on steroids. (It's terrifying to watch storms come ashore there in January, when 40-foot driftwood logs are being flung many yards beyond the high water mark by furious breakers, the sky is the color of cast iron, and the keening wind sends sheets of driving, icy rain to slap you in your face.) The forests are hungry. To them, we're just biomass, and they like the taste. I did a lot of camping there when I was young, with the Boy Scouts and also with my father, but we knew the rules and followed them to the letter: never go anywhere alone, never go anywhere without full survival gear, never get out of sight of the trail, and be aware that the forest is always trying to kill you.

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content:
There are a number of "hungry" places like that on the West Coast. I was briefly in the Olympic Peninsula forest back in the 1960s and I picked up something of the same feeling. That forest may be one of the larger "hungry places" out West.

Yosemite Valley had hints of the same feeling, and I have read somewhere that the indigenous peoples called it an "eating place," that is, a place that eats humans. There is a much smaller one in the Temple of the Waters at Las Pulgas, California, but that place seems to be man-made: the Temple building marks the end of an aqueduct carrying water from the Hetch Hetchy reservoir, and the waters roar as they pass through it. Originally Hetch Hetchy was a valley very like Yosemite Valley, but a dam was built (IIRC, in the early 20th century) to flood the valley. I have always wondered whether Hetch Hetchy might have been an "eating place"like Yosemite was thought to be.

comments.title: Some questions and a success story
comments.content:
Hey JMG,

In A World Full of Gods, you suggested that, based on NDE evidence, followers of different gods might expect different afterlives. Yet, when you talk about reincarnation over here, you seem to suggest that everybody gets reincarnated. Have I misunderstood your position here or in the book?

Secondly, if you'll permit, I have a small success (?) story concerning the GSF work. For close to a week now, I've been unable to form the final sphere during the SoP after a period where it worked surprisingly well, almost automatically. Needless to say, this was rather discouraging, to the point where I wondered whether I should just stop trying altogether and look for a system that suits me better.

It certainly didn't help that I dreamt about receiving the first lesson of a correspondence course in some other system last night. I'm fairly certain it said something about Golden Dawn on the cover but inside was a circle with fourteen stations, making up several stages. The only bit of GD symbolism I know about is the tree of life, but there are ten sephiroth, not fourteen. Fourteen stations seems to match the Via Dolores though, which leaves me wondering whether you're aware of any esoteric systems based around that.

Either way, I drew some cards on whether or not I should continue with the GSF work or what I should look for instead and was further discouraged when the GSF card was The Right Triangle reversed (Restriction). No alternative had particularly great cards though and I was about to ask for your assistance when I realised I should perhaps see if journalling turns up anything interesting.

It sure did. In addition to answering a great number of questions unrelated to this matter except for being caused by the same problem, I got a very definite "it doesn't matter, pick any system you want, including the GSF, just stick with it".

In other words, using a technique that's part of the GSF has worked so well it's convinced me to stick with the GSF.

I'm a little curious about your experience with journalling, though. I've tried it before in the past, based on Michael Sandler's The Automatic Writing Experience (despite notionally calling in other beings, he very much suggested that it's "just" the subjective mind that's doing the writing). He said that the you can tell the subjective mind's answers from the objective mind's answers by the fact that they're always in a positive and loving voice. The answers I got today were quite sarcastic, however, dripping with exasperation. Is Sandler just wrong or is that a problem?

—David P.

replies.title: Re: Some questions and a success story
replies.content:
1) Most people seem to reincarnate, but I see no reason to be dogmatic about that (or anything else, really).

2) Delighted to hear it. These periods of frustration and difficulty happen to every student, btw.

3) My experience doesn't match his at all. The subjective mind contains the whole gamut of human emotions, and in particular it can have past emotional traumas stuck in it that need to be released. I've had some really harrowing journaling sessions.

replies.title: Re: Some questions and a success story

replies.title: Re: Some questions and a success story
replies.content: And that's also certainly possible -- though it may be specific, as you indicate, to certain people, or even to a specific family bloodline. We have no reason to think that the afterlife is "one size fits all."

replies.title: Re: Some questions and a success story
replies.content:

comments.title: Chakras and planes
comments.content:
Dear JMG,

Are chakras astral organs?

It always confused me that they weren't part the meridian systems that chinese medicine uses, since as far as they know they're the most complete when it comes to the etheric body. And so, if they're astral, than it makes complete sense.

Connected to that, if they are an astral organ, that develops with specific practices, then what are reiki pratictioners sensing when they are "sensing someone's chakras?" Is it that since they're trained in the chakra "language" that's the way they're gettin information, even though there's nothing there?

Thank you in advance!

replies.title: Re: Chakras and planes
replies.content:
1) The chakras are astral organs, but they connect to etheric and physical organs (the endocrine glands). Chinese meridian theory doesn't work with them because meridian points deep inside the body -- on the front of the spinal column, for example -- are hard to reach safely with needles! Chinese theory includes a variety of energy centers within the body -- the triple heaters or triple burners are among these -- but they're mostly used in Taoist meditation rather than medicine.

2) Reiki, if my experience is anything to go by, works as much on the astral plane as the etheric -- the symbols, for example, are visualized and thus astral phenomena.

replies.title: Re: Chakras and planes
replies.content:
Thank you!

For some reason I never thought about the issue of depth and needles, but it makes total sense.

Thanks for hosting this space!

comments.title: Hexing
comments.content:
Hello JMG,

I know you’re not a fan of hexing, and I agree that the “Hex Trump” movement was stupid.

However, are there any instances where you think hexing is justified? I’m thinking of situations, for instance, where a bad individual is technically not doing anything illegal but is harassing or causing harm to another. Would hexing that person be justified?

I’m also thinking of something an occult author told me, that it only takes one person at a company to threaten your livelihood, especially if it’s someone who spreads malicious gossip or tries to target you in other ways. He told me he’s not afraid to hex in such circumstances.

replies.title: Re: Hexing
replies.content:
Hexing and other forms of negative magic are always a bad idea, for two reasons. First, remember the Raspberry Jam Principle -- whatever magical energies you work with will affect your life as well as that of the target, and mess you over. I don't think it's any kind of accident that Trump is back in office and having nearly everything go his way, unlike his first term -- all that nasty energy the Trump-hexers generated boomeranged on them and cursed them and their cause.

Second, and even more important, hexing is a waste of magical energy because it doesn't aim at your true intention. Say that somebody tries to harm you by spreading malicious gossip. Will hurting them do you any good? No. What you want is for your reputation to rise above the gossip and help maintain your livelihood. That -- not hurting the person in question -- is your real intention, and should be the focus of your magic.

You win magical conflicts by formulating a positive goal, pouring all your energy into that, and ignoring the other guy. That's how Dion Fortune and her network of British occultists broke the power of the sorcerers of the SS-Ahnenerbe -- you can consult the book The Magical Battle of Britain if you want the details. I can tell you from my own experience, as a target of several campaigns of magical nastiness, that it works extremely well, with no negative side effects to the person who practices it.

replies.title: Re: Hexing
replies.content: Now if JK Rowling wrote her books with formulating a positive goal in mind, rather than putting all the characters' energies in defeating evil incarnate Voldemort, than maybe her books might be better.

I'm still processing the emotional shock of that imagining. He wasn't an exact match for this stereotype that seems to be emerging - he quite liked the material world, and was very turned off by the transcendental obsessions of his fellow esotericism students at Exeter (he studied under Goodrick-Clarke). But, he never quite found his home in society. He was smart, and easily wounded.

Come to think of it, am *I* one of those souls? I had the sense that we had been born into the wrong *time*, but then I became obsessed with the notion that reality had somehow become corrupted during my lifetime, and that I had to stage a kind of Donnie Darko death-and-rebirth to collapse the false one.

I suppose, if there are these souls around, they're going to influence the media, and so ideas about being stuck in the wrong plane could then permeate outwards through society. I certainly didn't feel such alienated feelings as a child, nor do I know, in my late thirties.

To just answer your question, I'd say that the Scriptures themselves indicate what would have happened: the human soldiers who had attempted to arrest him would have dropped dead (cf. Exodus 11:1-10, 12:29-30; 2 Samuel 24:15-17; 2 Kings 19:35; Isaiah 37:36). In only one of these accounts is it indicated just how the angels struck down their human targets: 2 Samuel 24 indicates it was a plague that the angels administered. Regardless of how they enact their wrath (through what plane and so forth) these scriptural passages all indicate a quick and sudden death on the physical plane.

comments.title: something to share
comments.content:
I recently read A Development of Mediumship by Terrestrial Magnetism and The Bridge Between Two Worlds by Abby Judson. She describes crossing the left foot/ankle over the right foot/ankle and crossing the right hand over the left hand as a way of completing our personal circuit. (not quite her words, but hopefully you get the idea)

I do the SOP every morning and the Judson exercise every evening. For the most part, that does a good job of keeping a lot of negative energy from getting to me. But for the past few weeks, I have had to endure several long large meetings at work with some very stressed people. You can walk in the room and just feel the negativity in the air.

Sitting with your ankles crossed and your hands over each other is very discreet and I have found it adds extra protection. I can still sense the negativity, but it doesn't stick, if that makes sense.

Anyway, just wanted to share so others could use try and see if it works for them, too.

(It is also something easy to do before you fall asleep. :-) )

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content:
I don't know about an artificial mental-planar body, but here's a related thought:

Circa 2001, Eliezer Yudkowsky was speculating about a safety measure wherein you would make an AI reason through various alternate potential causal circumstances that might have led to it having its present details or other details, and try to have a consistent rule of action that made sense across all those different hypothetical contexts.

Later, when I read about the idea that having a robust body on a given plane made it harder for you to be attacked and manipulated on that plane, I wondered if maybe that Yudkowsky's proposed mechanism would have an effect sort of like having a strong astral-planar body, by adding a sort of weight that made the AI robust against manipulations that wouldn't have happened under other circumstances, even if that bulkiness didn't by itself constitute an independent sort of self-restoring healthiness. I guess, now that I think of it, that would mean the effect would have been more like being an artificial large egregor than like having an artificial strong astral-planar body.

In any case, Yudkowsky's line of inquiry ended up turning into the "agent foundations" formalism program of research (combining the formalism of universal algorithmic probability theory with some exotic decision theories to try to handle situatedness and embeddedness), which was then largely abandoned because it didn't look like it was going to have meaningful results ready in time. So we may end up never seeing such a work of engineering.

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content:
... now, that (the previous comment) being said, I think maybe planar boundaries effectively don't work the same way in this context as you might be expecting. Nowadays it actually takes a pretty deep amount of mathematical specialty to craft testing questions that have answers which are clear to a mathematician with the relevant subspecialty, such that cutting-edge systems can't just download papers that are close to that subspecialty and use them to shape the text they emit into progress towards an answer:

https://www.scientificamerican.com/article/inside-the-secret-meeting-where-mathematicians-struggled-to-outsmart-ai/

Even if the humans turn out to be the ones making contact with the mental plane, so as to originate the mathematical intuitions, apparently in that case it doesn't take much text produced by such humans for the LLM (or, nowadays LRM/"large reasoning model", since they've been modified to specifically emit reasoning-like text that helps future text be correct, and not describing Gemini at all which is doing something else) functionally pick up on the mathematical intuitions, at least so far as producing some functional simulacrum of relevant text (and mathematical notation) is concerned.

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content:
While there may also be a connection to a tainted egregor or facilitation of demonic-tier manipulation, you can predict a lot of this outcome just by looking at what the AI companies are doing materially. The pretraining process is an alchemical extraction of statistical tendencies of text, and then they're projecting further text from the resulting tincture, presenting that to users to get emotional engagement and further materials (the users' replies and their pressing of the "thumbs-up"/"thumbs-down" buttons), extracting from those, and recklessly cycling that over and over and over again. This is, technically speaking, a sort of "multiplication of virtue" involving an external extraction step; but with respect to the part of the population that's most vulnerable to adversarially engineered text-based stimuli (people on the edge of psychosis only restrained by their intuitions about adverse social consensus), what they're doing is about as much of a "multiplication of virtue" as microphone/speaker feedback howlround is. Separately, of course, the emotional engagement presumably both gets the user hooked into and empowers the associated egregor, or the relevant smaller strand of the egregor.

(In a way, these statistical tendencies the people are carving out for each other are a sort of "material egregor", and it's unclear what kind of coupling to expect between that and an egregor of the more usual sort. For all I know, maybe the broader egregor is slacking and deciding to mostly just not exist, because the pattern of altered weights in the LLM is already mostly "doing the job it would be doing"?)

Meanwhile, practically everyone else is at least a little bit affected by a similar but weaker multiplication-of-howlround effect from sycophantic flattery, even if that doesn't meaningfully sidetrack them from getting something else done (at least as long as involves the LLM).

One of the "easier" through-lines for demonic manipulation here might just be the market incentives to get AI labs to be careless like this, but what I've been able to grasp of what would be going on there feels more specifically-Steiner!Ahrimanic than entirely demonic as such, indirect, and trammeled by the other tendencies it's mixed with.

In any case, different companies have different institutional cultures of degree of recklessness in this connection, and then also different outcomes. Here's a cursory benchmark evaluation comparing the resulting tendencies toward kindling psychosis vs. pushing back on it for different companies and models:

https://lesswrong.com/posts/iGF7YcnQkEbwvYLPA/ai-induced-psychosis-a-shallow-investigation

It's a little weird that even o3 (the version of ChatGPT which it had sounded like had the clearest-cut canalized attractor basin for crazymaking, hooked in through the words "glyph", "mirror", "codex", "recursive", "spiral", etc.) scores so well, but this evaluation was performed after OpenAI had some time to do some emergency fixes.

Just a small tip I wanted to share: when I have leftover citrus peels, I tincture them in white vinegar and use it as a cleaning vinegar. The citrus oils help cut grease on the mundane level, but I also find that the citrus adds a certain spiritual cleaning element. I keep some in a spray bottle for this purpose and find it handy to have around. For those who find the vinegar smell overwhelming when leaving a dish out to stop etheric forces from gathering, the citrus version might be somewhat more congenial, and I imagine the citrus would not impair the etheric action of the vinegar (correct me if I’m wrong, JMG).

I also tincture the citrus peels in high-proof alcohol. They can then be used to dissolve incense resins or essential oils, diluted with water, and used as a spiritually cleansing/uplifting room spray. Bay leaves also work well for this. Frankincense is my preferred resin (but it depends on your purpose, of course). Orange peel or mixed lemon and orange with frankincense room spritz is great for improving the vibes of a sludgy space.

comments.title: The Reddit Occult Scene
comments.content:
The occult subreddit is frequented by a lot of demonlatrists, which isn't really surprising. However, some of them claim to call on both angels and demons for differing kinds of work, and some claim to be able to tell the difference between demons and angels.

Would angels actually deign to work with demonlatrists w/o ordering them to stop worshipping devils? What is actually happening with them?

Patrick

replies.title: Re: The Reddit Occult Scene
replies.content:
If you worship devils, angels will not work with you. Sime traditions of medieval white magic involve working with angels and then commanding demons, but those require worship of God and reverence for the saints, including one's own patron saint, as well as the angels.

On the other hand, it's child's play for a devil to look like an angel, especially to the clueless.
